summary: When I was about five or six, after my father passed during the 1918 flu epidemic, my mother brought us to Ocean Park, California. My great-aunt owned the Belclair Apartments there, and my mother managed the twenty-some unit resort complex right on the oceanfront. Growing up there was wonderful; we lived mostly in our bathing suits, swimming and playing on the wide white sandy beaches year-round. I have vivid memories of body surfing, making surfboards, and working summer jobs renting umbrellas and chairs to visitors.

I was born and spent my early childhood in St. Louis, Missouri, before moving to California. One of my earliest memories is the long train trip from St. Louis to Los Angeles, which took about four days and three nights. I didn’t enjoy school much back in St. Louis and was a bit reluctant to attend. Leaving St. Louis for the beaches of Ocean Park was a big change for me as a young boy.

Arriving in Los Angeles was a major turning point for me as a child. After the long train ride from St. Louis, we were met and taken to Ocean Park, part of the greater Los Angeles area. I attended schools in the Los Angeles region, including Washington Grammar and John Adams Junior High, and later Santa Monica High School. I also remember the 1932 Summer Olympics held in Los Angeles and visiting the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum with friends.

One memorable day on New Year's, my mother, brothers, and I went up to Pacific Palisades. I was wearing just a bathing suit and a beanie cap when a gentleman from England took my picture to send back home, showing how Californians dressed on January first. It was an unusual but exciting experience for me as a child growing up near the beach.

I recall a gentleman from London, England, who took my picture on New Year's Day while we were visiting Pacific Palisades. He wanted to show his friends back in England how people dressed in California on January first, which was quite different from their cold weather attire. It was a curious and amusing moment for me as a child.

After moving to California, I attended Washington Grammar School in Ocean Park. Unlike my earlier experience in St. Louis, I quickly made friends and enjoyed school much more. It was the start of my positive educational journey in California before moving on to John Adams Junior High and Santa Monica High School.

I went to John Adams Junior High School after Washington Grammar. It was part of my schooling in the Los Angeles area, where I became more involved in sports like basketball. This school helped me transition smoothly into high school life at Santa Monica High.

I graduated from Santa Monica High School in January 1931 as vice president of my class. While there, I played football, baseball, and basketball, and even tried pole vaulting, though that didn’t go well. One of my teachers was Miss Ruth Judd, who was quite a character and related to Winnie Ruth Judd, the infamous figure in a local murder case.

I had a newspaper route in Westwood, Los Angeles, where I sold about fifty-two papers. The homes were spread out, so I had to ride my bicycle several miles before starting the route. I remember one rainy day when I tried to ferry a subscriber's daughter across a two-foot deep ravine on my bike, which ended with us both falling into the water. It was a tough but memorable job during my youth.

As a child growing up in Ocean Park, I remember the filming of The Sheik of Araby right on the beach. Rudolph Valentino starred in it, and it was exciting to watch the movie production with tents set up and the use of reflective boards instead of flash cameras. It was a unique experience to see Hollywood at work so close to where I lived.

I recall seeing Rudolph Valentino during the filming of The Sheik of Araby on the Ocean Park beach. It was fascinating to watch him and the movie crew work without modern lighting, using reflective boards instead. Valentino was a big star, and his presence made the beach feel like a movie set for us kids.

Charlie Chase was a comedian who used to perform down at Ocean Park when I was growing up. His comedy acts were part of the entertainment we enjoyed in the area. It was fun to see local performers like him during those beach days.

To help support my family, my brothers and I sold newspapers in Ocean Park and nearby areas. I had a route in Westwood where I sold fifty-two papers, often riding my bike several miles before starting. Selling papers was a tough job, especially in the rain, but it taught me responsibility and gave me many memorable experiences.

As a child in grammar school, I remember reading newspaper headlines about the San Francisco Giants playing in the 1921 World Series. Those baseball games were big news and captured the attention of many, including myself. It was part of the sports culture I grew up with in California.

The 1921 World Series was a memorable event during my childhood, with the San Francisco Giants playing. I recall the excitement from newspaper headlines about the games. It was one of the early sports moments that stuck with me growing up in California.

I remember when the Graf Zeppelin flew over Ocean Park during my grammar school days. The whole school was let out to watch it pass overhead, which was quite an event. It was a rare and thrilling sight for us kids at the time.

During my grammar school years, I recall hearing about the Hindenburg disaster. The Graf Zeppelin and Hindenburg were both big news, and the school let us out to see the airships fly over Ocean Park. The Hindenburg's tragic end made a lasting impression on the community.

Clover Field was the local airport near Ocean Park where the round-the-world fliers landed around 1925. Our school let us out so we could go see the planes and the pilots, which was an exciting experience. Today, Clover Field is the site of the Douglas Aircraft Company, but back then it was mostly open fields.

The Douglas Aircraft Company now occupies what used to be Clover Field, where I saw the round-the-world fliers land in the mid-1920s. Back then, the area was mostly open fields and a local airport. It was fascinating to witness the early days of aviation and later see the growth of the aircraft industry there.

When I was in grammar school, Wilshire Boulevard was a modest forty-foot wide asphalt street lined with eucalyptus trees. It was nothing like the bustling, valuable property it has become today. I remember walking or biking along it during those early years in Los Angeles.

The 1932 Summer Olympics held in Los Angeles was a big event I remember well. The Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum was built and dedicated for the games. My friends and I took trolley cars from Ocean Park to watch the free Olympic qualifying events, though we accidentally took the wrong trolley on the way home and ended up touring the city.
cues: Ocean Park to Los Angeles trolley

summary: The Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum was built for the 1932 Olympics, and I had the chance to visit it with friends to watch the qualifying events. It was an impressive new structure and a source of pride for the city. The visit was part of a memorable day that ended with us accidentally riding the wrong trolley car.

Miss Ruth Judd was one of my history teachers at Santa Monica High School around 1930. She was a large, blunt, and very learned woman who made a strong impression on me. She once said that every twenty years or so, a war is needed to reduce the population, which was a striking comment for a teacher to make.

Winnie Ruth Judd was a notorious figure related to my teacher Ruth Judd. Shortly after I graduated in 1931, Winnie Ruth Judd was involved in a gruesome murder case where she decapitated two women and hid them in trunks. I recall hearing that she was later released and was living under supervision, which was a strange and unsettling story connected to my school days.

After graduating high school, I attended Santa Monica Junior College for one year, taking a business course. At the time, it was a new school, much smaller and in a different location than it is today. It was a stepping stone before I started working during the Depression era.

I worked for William F. Harrah in Venice, California, during the Depression. He ran a game called the Circle Game, which had thirty-three stools, and I was hired part-time on weekends and holidays. It was a tough time economically, but this job gave me some income and experience.

Venice, California, was where I worked for William F. Harrah at the Circle Game during the Depression. It was a lively beach community not far from Ocean Park where I grew up. Working there gave me a chance to earn money in tough times and experience a different side of the Los Angeles coastal area.
